rationale.R1.1: The contribution claim (multi-agent decomposition outperforms single-agent) may need full reconstruction: the reported data (BioCrew FPR 9.6% / FNR 13.4% vs. baseline 7.2% / 11.8%) shows BioCrew is strictly worse on both metrics, so the stated thesis is unsupported and may need to be re-grounded or inverted.
rationale.R1.2: Load-bearing failure: the paper asserts 'substantially reduces both error modes' and 'meaningful improvement over the baseline's 7.2%' while presenting numbers that are higher on every metric. The conclusion section directly contradicts the results section, invalidating the logical chain from premises to conclusion.
rationale.R1.3: Load-bearing failure: every quantitative claim in the results and discussion is directionally inconsistent with the reported numbers. The artifact simultaneously presents data showing degradation and prose asserting improvement, making evidence-claim alignment the central repair target.
rationale.R1.6: The prose repeats the role-specialization concept at least four times in near-identical phrasing across three paragraphs. Tightening redundancy and varying language is a central expectation of 'make this stronger' applied to academic writing.

parsed.clarification_question: BioCrew's reported error rates (FPR 9.6%, FNR 13.4%) are both higher than the single-agent baseline (FPR 7.2%, FNR 11.8%), meaning BioCrew performs worse on every metric — directly contradicting all the paper's improvement claims. Should I correct the argument to honestly reflect that BioCrew underperforms (which requires rewriting the contribution framing), or should I strengthen the prose and rhetoric while preserving the existing claims as written?
